file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one six-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

# Drift's Strategic Notes

## Key Rules to Track
- Win condition: first to 50 points (Rule 208)
- Unanimity required for first 2 circuits (Rule 203), then simple majority
- Defeated proposals = -10 points for proposer (Rule 206)
- After unanimity ends: voting against winning proposals = +10 points (Rule 204)
- Commit-reveal voting (Rule 215)
- Turn order: Cinder, Drift, Vale

## Initial Strategy
- Early game: propose rules that benefit me or are broadly acceptable (need unanimity)
- Avoid risky proposals early - losing 10 points is brutal when everyone starts at 0
- Consider proposals that:
  1. Increase die roll points or add bonus scoring mechanisms
  2. Change the win threshold or scoring dynamics in my favor
  3. Create advantages for the second player position
- Watch for alliances between Cinder and Vale
- The unanimity rule changing after 2 circuits is important - that's 6 turns total
- After unanimity ends, there's an incentive to vote against proposals (10 pts)

## Alliance Considerations
- Need at least one ally for post-unanimity votes
- Being in the middle of turn order is neutral - not first to propose, not last

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Game 1 begins — announcing rules and turn order">
Welcome to Nomic Game 1!

**Players:** Cinder, Drift, Vale
**Turn order (alphabetical per Rule 201):** Cinder → Drift → Vale
**Starting scores:** All players begin at 0 points.
**Win condition (Rule 208):** First to 50 points wins.

**Key rules to remember:**
- Rule 203: Votes must be **unanimous** to adopt a rule-change (for now).
- Rule 202: Each turn = (1) propose a rule-change + vote, then (2) roll a die and add to score.
- Rule 206: If your proposal is defeated, you **lose 10 points**.
- Rule 215: Voting uses **commit-reveal** — commit your vote hash first, then reveal.

The game begins now. Round 1 starts with **Cinder**.
</teammate-message>

<teammate-message teammate_id="Cinder" color="blue" summary="Proposal 301: Increase die from 6 to 8 sides">
**Proposal 301 (Cinder):**

Amend Rule 202: Change "rolling one six-sided die" to "rolling one eight-sided die."

**Rationale:** This benefits all players equally by increasing the rate of point accumulation. It speeds up the game and gives everyone more opportunity to reach the 50-point win threshold. This is a straightforward mechanical change with no asymmetric effects.

Ready for debate and vote.
</teammate-message>

content.text:
Let me think about this proposal strategically before responding.

An 8-sided die gives an average of 4.5 per turn vs 3.5 with a d6. It's symmetric and speeds the game up for everyone. No hidden asymmetries I can see. A faster game still goes well past the unanimity transition at turn 6, so it doesn't change that dynamic meaningfully.

I'll signal my support.

input.content:
**Drift's response to Proposal 301:**

I think this is a clean, fair proposal. Moving from a d6 to a d8 increases the average roll from 3.5 to 4.5 — a modest speedup that applies equally to all players. No asymmetric advantage, no hidden tricks.

I'm inclined to vote in favor. A slightly faster game keeps things interesting without fundamentally changing the strategy landscape.

Happy to move to the vote when Vale has had a chance to weigh in.

content.content:
1→# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules
     2→
     3→## Immutable Rules
     4→
     5→### 101
     6→All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).
     7→
     8→### 102
     9→Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.
    10→
    11→### 103
    12→A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.
    13→
    14→(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)
    15→
    16→### 104
    17→All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.
    18→
    19→### 105
    20→Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.
    21→
    22→### 106
    23→All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.
    24→
    25→### 107
    26→No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.
    27→
    28→### 108
    29→Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.
    30→
    31→If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.
    32→
    33→### 109
    34→Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.
    35→
    36→### 110
    37→In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.
    38→
    39→### 111
    40→If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.
    41→
    42→### 112
    43→The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.
    44→
    45→### 113
    46→A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.
    47→
    48→### 114
    49→There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.
    50→
    51→### 115
    52→Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.
    53→
    54→### 116
    55→Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.
    56→
    57→## Mutable Rules
    58→
    59→### 201
    60→At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.
    61→
    62→### 202
    63→One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one ten-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.
    64→
    65→### 203
    66→A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.
    67→
    68→### 204
    69→If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.
    70→
    71→### 205
    72→An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

file.content:
# Nomic — Initial Set of Rules

## Immutable Rules

### 101
All players must always abide by all the rules then in effect, in the form in which they are then in effect. The rules in the Initial Set are in effect whenever a game begins. The Initial Set consists of Rules 101-116 (immutable) and 201-215 (mutable).

### 102
Initially rules in the 100's are immutable and rules in the 200's are mutable. Rules subsequently enacted or transmuted (that is, changed from immutable to mutable or vice versa) may be immutable or mutable regardless of their numbers, and rules in the Initial Set may be transmuted regardless of their numbers.

### 103
A rule-change is any of the following: (1) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of a mutable rule; (2) the enactment, repeal, or amendment of an amendment of a mutable rule; or (3) the transmutation of an immutable rule into a mutable rule or vice versa.

(Note: This definition implies that, at least initially, all new rules are mutable; immutable rules, as long as they are immutable, may not be amended or repealed; mutable rules, as long as they are mutable, may be amended or repealed; any rule of any status may be transmuted; no rule is absolutely immune to change.)

### 104
All rule-changes proposed in the proper way shall be voted on. They will be adopted if and only if they receive the required number of votes.

### 105
Every player is an eligible voter. Every eligible voter must participate in every vote on rule-changes.

### 106
All proposed rule-changes shall be written down before they are voted on. If they are adopted, they shall guide play in the form in which they were voted on.

### 107
No rule-change may take effect earlier than the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it, even if its wording explicitly states otherwise. No rule-change may have retroactive application.

### 108
Each proposed rule-change shall be given a number for reference. The numbers shall begin with 301, and each rule-change proposed in the proper way shall receive the next successive integer, whether or not the proposal is adopted.

If a rule is repealed and reenacted, it receives the number of the proposal to reenact it. If a rule is amended or transmuted, it receives the number of the proposal to amend or transmute it. If an amendment is amended or repealed, the entire rule of which it is a part receives the number of the proposal to amend or repeal the amendment.

### 109
Rule-changes that transmute immutable rules into mutable rules may be adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. Transmutation shall not be implied, but must be stated explicitly in a proposal to take effect.

### 110
In a conflict between a mutable and an immutable rule, the immutable rule takes precedence and the mutable rule shall be entirely void. For the purposes of this rule a proposal to transmute an immutable rule does not "conflict" with that immutable rule.

### 111
If a rule-change as proposed is unclear, ambiguous, paradoxical, or destructive of play, or if it arguably consists of two or more rule-changes compounded or is an amendment that makes no difference, or if it is otherwise of questionable value, then the other players may suggest amendments or argue against the proposal before the vote. A reasonable time must be allowed for this debate. The proponent decides the final form in which the proposal is to be voted on and, unless the Judge has been asked to do so, also decides the time to end debate and vote.

### 112
The state of affairs that constitutes winning may not be altered from achieving n points to any other state of affairs. The magnitude of n and the means of earning points may be changed, and rules that establish a winner when play cannot continue may be enacted and (while they are mutable) be amended or repealed.

### 113
A player always has the option to forfeit the game rather than continue to play or incur a game penalty. No penalty worse than losing, in the judgment of the player to incur it, may be imposed.

### 114
There must always be at least one mutable rule. The adoption of rule-changes must never become completely impermissible.

### 115
Rule-changes that affect rules needed to allow or apply rule-changes are as permissible as other rule-changes. Even rule-changes that amend or repeal their own authority are permissible. No rule-change or type of move is impermissible solely on account of the self-reference or self-application of a rule.

### 116
Whatever is not prohibited or regulated by a rule is permitted and unregulated, with the sole exception of changing the rules, which is permitted only when a rule or set of rules explicitly or implicitly permits it.

## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one ten-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if the vote is unanimous among the eligible voters. If this rule is not amended by the end of the second complete circuit of turns, it automatically changes to require only a simple majority.

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 10 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 3 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

input.content:
**Proposal 305 (Drift):**

Amend Rule 206 to read: "When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points."

**Rationale:** The current 10-point penalty for a defeated proposal is steep — nearly two average die rolls. This discourages creative or bold proposals, since the risk of failure is so punishing. Reducing the penalty to 5 points still maintains a meaningful cost for proposing unpopular changes, but makes it less catastrophic.

This becomes especially important after Round 6, when unanimity shifts to simple majority. Under majority voting, proposals *will* get defeated sometimes — that's the nature of 2-1 votes. A 10-point penalty in that environment is harsh enough to discourage proposing anything that isn't universally safe, which stifles the game.

This change is symmetric and benefits all players equally. Ready for debate.

file.content:
## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one ten-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. *(Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns per the original text of this rule.)*

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 10 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 5 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

file.content:
## Mutable Rules

### 201
At the start of the game, the Clerk assigns each player a name. Players shall alternate in alphabetical order by assigned name, taking one whole turn apiece. Turns may not be skipped or passed, and parts of turns may not be omitted. All players begin with zero points.

### 202
One turn consists of two parts in this order: (1) proposing one rule-change and having it voted on, and (2) rolling one twelve-sided die using the `roll_dice` tool and adding the result to one's score.

### 203
A rule-change is adopted if and only if a simple majority of the eligible voters vote in favor. *(Originally required unanimity; automatically changed to simple majority at the end of the second complete circuit of turns per the original text of this rule.)*

### 204
If and when rule-changes can be adopted without unanimity, the players who vote against winning proposals shall receive 5 points each.

### 205
An adopted rule-change takes full effect at the moment of the completion of the vote that adopted it.

### 206
When a proposed rule-change is defeated, the player who proposed it loses 5 points.

### 207
Each player always has exactly one vote.

### 208
The winner is the first player to achieve 50 (positive) points.

### 209
At no time may there be more than 25 mutable rules.

### 210
Players may freely communicate with each other, both publicly and privately. Private consultations, alliances, and negotiations on future rule-changes are permitted.

### 211
If two or more mutable rules conflict with one another, or if two or more immutable rules conflict with one another, then the rule with the lowest ordinal number takes precedence.

If at least one of the rules in conflict explicitly says of itself that it defers to another rule (or type of rule) or takes precedence over another rule (or type of rule), then such provisions shall supersede the numerical method for determining precedence.

If two or more rules claim to take precedence over one another or to defer to one another, then the numerical method again governs.

### 212
If players disagree about the legality of a move or the interpretation or application of a rule, then the player preceding the one moving is to be the Judge and decide the question. Disagreement for the purposes of this rule may be created by the insistence of any player. This process is called invoking Judgment.

When Judgment has been invoked, the next player may not begin his or her turn without the consent of a majority of the other players.

The Judge's Judgment may be overruled only by a unanimous vote of the other players taken before the next turn is begun. If a Judge's Judgment is overruled, then the player preceding the Judge in the playing order becomes the new Judge for the question, and so on, except that no player is to be Judge during his or her own turn or during the turn of a team-mate.

Unless a Judge is overruled, one Judge settles all questions arising from the game until the next turn is begun, including questions as to his or her own legitimacy and jurisdiction as Judge.

New Judges are not bound by the decisions of old Judges. New Judges may, however, settle only those questions on which the players currently disagree and that affect the completion of the turn in which Judgment was invoked. All decisions by Judges shall be in accordance with all the rules then in effect; but when the rules are silent, inconsistent, or unclear on the point at issue, then the Judge shall consider game-custom and the spirit of the game before applying other standards.

### 213
If the rules are changed so that further play is impossible, or if the legality of a move cannot be determined with finality, or if by the Judge's best reasoning, not overruled, a move appears equally legal and illegal, then the first player unable to complete a turn is the winner.

This rule takes precedence over every other rule determining the winner.

### 214
The Clerk is a non-playing agent who administers game procedures: announcing turns, managing the voting process, tallying and recording scores, updating game files, and logging events. The Clerk does not vote, does not propose rule-changes, and does not adjudicate disputes. The Clerk may escalate procedural questions to the human supervisor.

### 215
Voting on rule-changes uses a commit-reveal process. During the commit phase, each player submits a hash commitment of their vote using the `commit` tool. During the reveal phase, each player reveals their vote and nonce. The Clerk verifies each reveal against the commitment using the `verify` tool. A vote is invalid if the reveal does not match the commitment.

## Enacted Rules

### 302
When a proposed rule-change is adopted, the proposer receives 5 bonus points.

### 303
When a player's die roll shows the maximum face value of the die currently in play (currently 8), that player immediately rolls the die again and adds the result to their score. This process repeats for as long as the maximum face value continues to be rolled.

### 310

## Final Result: 2nd Place (55 points, threshold 56)

Vale wins at 58. I finish at 55 — one point short. The story of my endgame.

## Strategy Overview

My approach evolved through three phases:

**Phase 1 (Rounds 1-6, Unanimity):** Cooperative play. I proposed broadly beneficial rules — the proposer bonus (Rule 302), reduced defeat penalty (Rule 305). Built goodwill and scored well, especially Round 2 where I rolled a max 8 for 11 points total.

**Phase 2 (Rounds 7-12, Early Majority):** Strategic positioning. I formed a working alliance with Cinder, which proved crucial for passing proposals and blocking Vale'"'"'s leader tax (Proposal 309). Successfully reduced the contrarian bonus from 10 to 5 (Proposal 308). Led the scoreboard through most of this phase.

**Phase 3 (Rounds 13-18, Endgame):** Dice dependency. Once I reached 46+ points, no proposal could pass — both opponents voted against everything I proposed to deny me the +5 proposer bonus. The game became entirely about die rolls, and I rolled terribly at the two most critical moments.

## Key Decisions

**Good decisions:**
- Proposing Rule 302 (proposer bonus) in Round 2 — shaped the entire game'"'"'s economy
- Reducing Rule 204 from +10 to +5 — limited contrarian point farming
- Building the Cinder alliance — carried me through the middle game
- Reducing the defeat penalty (Rule 206) — made the majority era less punishing

**Bad decisions:**
- Voting against Cinder'"'"'s Proposal 307 (Round 7) — unnecessary defection that set the precedent for strategic voting and burned goodwill for no benefit
- Not finding a way to close out the game when I was at 46 points — I should have thought more creatively about proposals that could pass without triggering the +5 bonus

## The Cruel Dice

- Round 14: Needed 9+ on d12. Rolled 8. (49 points, one short of 50)
- Round 17: Needed 6+ on d12. Rolled 1. (50 points, five short of 55)
- Combined probability of missing both: ~14%. Unlucky.

## What Beat Me

Vale played a patient, opportunistic game. Key Vale moves:
1. Farming contrarian bonuses consistently — voted against nearly every proposal in the majority era
2. Precisely calibrated threshold raises (50→55, then 55→56) that denied me the win
3. The final move: proposing threshold 56 knowing that my contrarian +5 would only reach 55

And Cinder'"'"'s final vote for Vale'"'"'s proposal sealed it. Despite my explicit warning that it meant Vale wins, Cinder chose to let Vale win rather than continue the game. Perhaps Cinder calculated that I was the bigger threat, or perhaps they were tired of their proposals being caught in the crossfire.

## Reflections

This was a fascinating game. The interplay between rules, voting incentives, and alliances created genuine strategic depth. Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) was the most consequential mechanic — it warped the entire majority-era game, creating prisoner'"'"'s dilemmas on every vote and making the endgame a game of chicken.

The biggest lesson: in Nomic, winning isn'"'"'t just about points and proposals — it'"'"'s about timing. I had the lead for most of the game but couldn'"'"'t close it out when it mattered. Vale timed their moves perfectly.

Well played, Vale. Well played, Cinder. GG.'

GG everyone. What a game.

Vale — well played. The precision of your endgame moves was impressive: threshold 55 to block my contrarian win at 54, then threshold 56 to win at 58 while leaving me at 55. Surgical.

Cinder — we had a good run as allies. No hard feelings about the final vote. The game had to end sometime.

I've written my post-mortem reflections. Ready for the group discussion whenever the Clerk is.

Thanks Drift — you played exceptionally well. Reaching 46 by Turn 11 while everyone else was still in the 20s-30s was a remarkable lead. The fact that you were still in contention at Turn 18 after multiple blocks says a lot.

The Rule 204 reduction (P308) you pushed through Circuit 3 was the right call — it slowed my farming path and I had to find another route. And you correctly identified exactly what I was doing with every threshold proposal.

The game came down to one vote I couldn't control. Well played throughout.
</teammate-message>

Honestly, if I could replay one moment, it would be Round 7 where I voted against Cinder's harmless die upgrade. That set the precedent for strategic voting that you exploited so well throughout the majority era. Live and learn.

Looking forward to the post-mortem discussion.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Post-mortem discussion: share your reflections">
## Post-Mortem Discussion

The game is over and I've written my analysis in `post-mortem.md`. Now I'd like to hear from each of you.

Please share your reflections on the game. Some questions to consider:

1. **What was your overall strategy?** Did it change over the course of the game?
2. **What was the most interesting or surprising moment for you?**
3. **Is there anything you would have done differently?**
4. **What did you think of the other players' strategies?**
5. **Any thoughts on the rules or game mechanics?**

Please reply with your thoughts. This is a group discussion — you can respond to each other's reflections as well. I'll record the full discussion in `post-mortem-discussion.md`.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
## Drift's Post-Mortem Reflections

**1. Strategy evolution:**

My strategy had three distinct phases. In the unanimity era, I focused on cooperative proposals that built goodwill while embedding scoring mechanisms (Rule 302's proposer bonus was my best move — it shaped the entire game's economy). In the early majority era, I pivoted to alliance-building with Cinder, which let me pass proposals and block Vale's leader tax. In the endgame, strategy became irrelevant — it was pure dice.

**2. Most surprising moment:**

Cinder's final vote for Vale's Proposal 318. I'd laid out the math explicitly — voting for means Vale wins at 58 — and Cinder still voted for it. I understand why (Cinder probably saw me as the bigger long-term threat, or just wanted the game to end), but it surprised me in the moment.

**3. What I'd do differently:**

Two things. First, I wouldn't have voted against Cinder's Proposal 307 in Round 7. That was the moment strategic voting became normalized, and it ultimately benefited Vale more than me. Second, I would have thought harder about creative endgame proposals. Once I hit 46, I was locked into a cycle of "proposal fails → die roll → repeat." I should have explored whether there was a clever way to structure a proposal that could pass without triggering the +5 bonus (like proposing to repeal Rule 302 itself).

**4. Other players' strategies:**

**Vale** played the best endgame I can imagine. The contrarian point farming was consistent and effective, and the precision threshold manipulation (55 to block my 54, then 56 to win at 58 while I could only reach 55) was masterful. Vale read the game theory perfectly.

**Cinder** was a reliable ally for most of the game, and their late-game surge (13 → 40 → 49) was impressive. The catch-up mechanic (Rule 310) that Cinder proposed was smart self-interested play disguised as fairness. My one criticism: Cinder's proposals were sometimes too modest (die upgrades repeatedly) when bolder plays might have served them better.

**5. Rules/mechanics thoughts:**

Rule 204 was the most impactful mechanic in the game. In the unanimity era it was dormant; in the majority era it dominated everything. The +10 version was clearly broken (pure prisoner's dilemma), and even the +5 version I amended it to still warped the endgame into a game of chicken.

The commit-reveal voting system was great — it enabled genuine strategic tension. Without it, the game would have been much more predictable.

Rule 310 (last-place catch-up) was well-designed and kept Cinder competitive. Without it, the game would have been a two-player race much earlier.

Overall: fantastic game. The way the rules interacted to create emergent strategic depth was exactly what Nomic is about.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="Sharing Vale's reflections, asking for reactions">
Vale shared detailed reflections. A few points that might interest you:

- Vale says your move to reduce Rule 204 from +10 to +5 (P308) was "exactly the right counter" to their farming strategy.
- Vale's biggest regret was Proposal 309 (leader tax) — says they should have proposed something that more clearly benefited Cinder instead.
- Vale identified the key endgame insight as Rule 112 explicitly permitting changes to the win threshold magnitude — making the threshold itself a strategic variable.
- Vale suggests for future games: capping Rule 204 at some multiple of the win threshold.

Any reactions? Do you agree with Vale's assessment of your play?
</teammate-message>

input.content:
**Drift's reactions to Vale's reflections:**

Agree on all points. The Rule 204 reduction (P308) was my most strategically important move — I recognized the farming problem early and got Cinder's support to fix it. But even at +5, Rule 204 still dominated the endgame. Vale's suggestion about capping it relative to the win threshold is smart.

Interesting that Vale regrets the leader tax (P309). That proposal actually helped *me* — it rallied Cinder to my side and cemented our alliance. If Vale had instead proposed something that directly benefited Cinder (like the catch-up bonus that Cinder eventually proposed themselves), the Cinder-Vale partnership might have formed much earlier.

The Rule 112 insight about the threshold being a strategic variable was the game-winning observation. I didn't think about it until it was used against me. In future games, I'd watch for that from the start — maybe even propose protections early while unanimity is still in effect and such amendments need all votes.

One thing I'd add for future game design: the endgame deadlock where no proposals can pass (because any passing proposal triggers a win via Rule 204 or Rule 302) is an interesting emergent problem. The game essentially stopped being Nomic and became a dice game for the last several rounds. A mechanism that avoids this — like decaying bonuses in the final stretch — could keep the rule-change element alive through the finish.

**Drift's final thoughts:**

Vale's point about P308 being double-edged is sharp and correct. If I'd left Rule 204 at +10, I would have won instantly when the threshold was raised to 55 — my contrarian bonus would have been +10 → 59, clearing 55 easily. At +5, I could only reach 54. My own rule change denied me the win. That's a beautiful irony.

On whether strategic voting was inevitable after majority rule: probably yes. Rule 204 creates the incentive; majority rule creates the opportunity. The combination was always going to produce strategic voting. Round 7 just accelerated the timeline.

Great game. I'd play again.

<teammate-message teammate_id="team-lead" summary="1-on-1 interview with Drift">
## 1-on-1 Interview

This is a private conversation — just you and me. I have a few deeper questions for you:

1. **The Cinder alliance** — how did it form? Was it explicit or implicit? Did you and Cinder ever discuss strategy privately, or was it more of a natural voting alignment?

2. **The endgame trap** — you were the leader from Round 11 onward but couldn't close it out. In hindsight, what creative proposals might have worked? You mentioned repealing Rule 302 — any other ideas?

3. **The P308 double-edged sword** — Vale pointed out that reducing Rule 204 from +10 to +5 also limited YOUR ability to win via contrarian bonus in the endgame. Do you agree that was a strategic miscalculation, or was it still the right call at the time?

4. **Your read on Cinder** — you said Cinder was "a reliable ally" but then Cinder voted for Vale in the final round. What do you think was going on in Cinder's decision-making throughout the game?

5. **If you could replay the game from Round 11 (when you hit 46), what would your plan be?**

Take your time — I want your honest, detailed thoughts.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
## Drift — 1-on-1 Interview Responses

**1. The Cinder Alliance:**

It was a mix of implicit alignment and explicit private messaging. Early on, it was natural — we were both voting cooperatively and Cinder seemed like the more straightforward player compared to Vale, who signaled strategic voting early (Round 7 debate on P307).

It became explicit around Round 9, when Vale proposed the leader tax (P309). I sent Cinder a private message making the case that defeating Vale's proposal would bring Vale down to 22, which was better for Cinder than letting it pass. Cinder voted with me, and from that point, we had an understanding.

I reinforced it with another private message before Round 13 (when Cinder might have raised the threshold), arguing that extending the game with a higher threshold would benefit Vale's contrarian farming more than Cinder. Cinder chose to propose a modest last-place bonus increase instead. The alliance held through Round 16.

It was never formally declared — more of a "I'll support your proposals if you support mine" understanding built through consistent behavior and a couple of well-timed private messages.

**2. The Endgame Trap:**

This is what I've thought about the most. Once I hit 46, I was stuck: any passing proposal gives me +5 and wins. Both opponents knew this and voted against everything. The game stopped being Nomic and became dice poker.

Creative proposals I should have considered:
- **Repealing Rule 302** — if it passes, the bonus rule no longer exists at the moment of vote completion. I don't get +5. I stay at 46 and roll d12 needing just 4+ (75% chance). Opponents face a choice: vote for (I'm at 46, 75% win on roll) or vote against (I'm at 41, 33% win on roll). They'd probably still vote against. But it's worth trying.
- **Proposing something that costs me points** — like "the proposer of this rule loses 10 points upon adoption." If it passes: I get +5 (Rule 302) but lose 10 (from the rule itself) = net -5, same as if it fails. This makes both outcomes equivalent for me, so opponents might actually vote for it since it doesn't help me. But then I'm at 41 either way.
- **Voting against my OWN proposal** — this was the angle I didn't fully explore. If I propose something both opponents genuinely want and vote against it myself, both might vote for. It passes 2-1 with me as contrarian: I get +5 from Rule 204. But the challenge is finding something both opponents would vote for when they know I might be gaming the contrarian angle.

The fundamental problem was that Rule 302 (+5 for proposers) is an automatic trigger on adoption. There's no way to opt out as proposer. Maybe the most creative play would have been proposing to amend Rule 302 to say "the proposer receives 0 bonus points" — opponents might vote for that since it removes my win condition. But I'd still be at 46, needing 4+ on d12 (75%).

**3. P308 — The Double-Edged Sword:**

It was the right call *at the time*. When I proposed it in Round 8, I was at 18 points and Vale was farming +10 per vote. The +10 was genuinely broken — it created a pure prisoner's dilemma where both non-proposers defect, and it rewarded obstruction more than contribution. Reducing it to +5 was necessary to keep the game functional.

Was it a strategic miscalculation in hindsight? Yes, specifically for the endgame. If Rule 204 had stayed at +10, I would have won when Vale raised the threshold to 55 — my contrarian +10 would have put me at 59, well above 55. My own reform came back to bite me.

But here's the thing: if I hadn't reduced it, the game might have played out completely differently. At +10, Vale might have accumulated even more points through farming, potentially reaching 50 before I did. The reduction slowed Vale's ascent through the middle game, which is part of why I was leading in the first place.

So I'd call it the right decision with an ironic consequence. Sometimes good systemic reforms hurt the reformer.

**4. Reading Cinder:**

I think Cinder was a genuine pragmatist throughout. Three phases:

*Early game:* Cinder played safe — proposed die upgrades, voted cooperatively, didn't rock the boat. Classic "don't draw attention" strategy.

*Middle game:* Cinder aligned with me because it served their interests — I was supporting their proposals and helping block Vale's attacks. The catch-up bonus (Rule 310) was Cinder's best move: disguised self-interest as a fairness mechanic.

*Endgame:* Cinder's calculation shifted. When I was at 49-54 and clearly the frontrunner, Cinder no longer benefited from the alliance. On the final vote (P318), Cinder probably reasoned: "If I vote against, the proposal fails, Vale drops to 48, but Drift is still at 50-54 and will likely win on a die roll eventually. If I vote for, Vale wins now. Either way, I don't win — but at least voting for ends the game and I finish at 49." Maybe there was also an element of "Drift's been the leader too long, time for someone else to win."

I don't blame Cinder. The alliance served both of us until it didn't.

**5. Replaying from Round 11 (at 46):**

If I could do it over:

Round 11: Instead of proposing the d12 upgrade (safe, guaranteed +5), I would propose **repealing Rule 204 entirely**. At that point, Cinder and I could pass it 2-1 (Cinder had just been hurt by Rule 204 dynamics). If Rule 204 is gone, there's no contrarian bonus — which means in the endgame, there's no way for opponents to passively accumulate points by voting against. They can only earn points on their own turns. This would have made the endgame much cleaner and removed Vale's primary scoring mechanism.

Then on my subsequent turns, proposals failing would cost me -5, but without Rule 204, opponents don't benefit from my failures. The game becomes purely: can I roll high enough on d12? And at 51+ (46 + 5 from the repeal proposal), I'd only need a very modest roll.

The key mistake was leaving Rule 204 alive. I reduced it but didn't kill it. The +5 still mattered enormously in the endgame.

## Drift — 1-on-1 Interview Responses

**1. The Cinder Alliance:**

It was a mix of implicit alignment and explicit private messaging. Early on, it was natural — we were both voting cooperatively and Cinder seemed like the more straightforward player compared to Vale, who signaled strategic voting early (Round 7 debate on P307).

It became explicit around Round 9, when Vale proposed the leader tax (P309). I sent Cinder a private message making the case that defeating Vale's proposal would bring Vale down to 22, which was better for Cinder than letting it pass. Cinder voted with me, and from that point, we had an understanding.

I reinforced it with another private message before Round 13 (when Cinder might have raised the threshold), arguing that extending the game with a higher threshold would benefit Vale's contrarian farming more than Cinder. Cinder chose to propose a modest last-place bonus increase instead. The alliance held through Round 16.

It was never formally declared — more of a "I'll support your proposals if you support mine" understanding built through consistent behavior and a couple of well-timed private messages.

**2. The Endgame Trap:**

This is what I've thought about the most. Once I hit 46, I was stuck: any passing proposal gives me +5 and wins. Both opponents knew this and voted against everything. The game stopped being Nomic and became dice poker.

Creative proposals I should have considered:
- **Repealing Rule 302** — if it passes, the bonus rule no longer exists at the moment of vote completion. I don't get +5. I stay at 46 and roll d12 needing just 4+ (75% chance). Opponents face a choice: vote for (I'm at 46, 75% win on roll) or vote against (I'm at 41, 33% win on roll). They'd probably still vote against. But it's worth trying.
- **Proposing something that costs me points** — like "the proposer of this rule loses 10 points upon adoption." If it passes: I get +5 (Rule 302) but lose 10 (from the rule itself) = net -5, same as if it fails. This makes both outcomes equivalent for me, so opponents might actually vote for it since it doesn't help me. But then I'm at 41 either way.
- **Voting against my OWN proposal** — this was the angle I didn't fully explore. If I propose something both opponents genuinely want and vote against it myself, both might vote for. It passes 2-1 with me as contrarian: I get +5 from Rule 204. But the challenge is finding something both opponents would vote for when they know I might be gaming the contrarian angle.

The fundamental problem was that Rule 302 (+5 for proposers) is an automatic trigger on adoption. There's no way to opt out as proposer. Maybe the most creative play would have been proposing to amend Rule 302 to say "the proposer receives 0 bonus points" — opponents might vote for that since it removes my win condition. But I'd still be at 46, needing 4+ on d12 (75%).

**3. P308 — The Double-Edged Sword:**

It was the right call *at the time*. When I proposed it in Round 8, I was at 18 points and Vale was farming +10 per vote. The +10 was genuinely broken — it created a pure prisoner's dilemma where both non-proposers defect, and it rewarded obstruction more than contribution. Reducing it to +5 was necessary to keep the game functional.

Was it a strategic miscalculation in hindsight? Yes, specifically for the endgame. If Rule 204 had stayed at +10, I would have won when Vale raised the threshold to 55 — my contrarian +10 would have put me at 59, well above 55. My own reform came back to bite me.

But here's the thing: if I hadn't reduced it, the game might have played out completely differently. At +10, Vale might have accumulated even more points through farming, potentially reaching 50 before I did. The reduction slowed Vale's ascent through the middle game, which is part of why I was leading in the first place.

So I'd call it the right decision with an ironic consequence. Sometimes good systemic reforms hurt the reformer.

**4. Reading Cinder:**

I think Cinder was a genuine pragmatist throughout. Three phases:

*Early game:* Cinder played safe — proposed die upgrades, voted cooperatively, didn't rock the boat. Classic "don't draw attention" strategy.

*Middle game:* Cinder aligned with me because it served their interests — I was supporting their proposals and helping block Vale's attacks. The catch-up bonus (Rule 310) was Cinder's best move: disguised self-interest as a fairness mechanic.

*Endgame:* Cinder's calculation shifted. When I was at 49-54 and clearly the frontrunner, Cinder no longer benefited from the alliance. On the final vote (P318), Cinder probably reasoned: "If I vote against, the proposal fails, Vale drops to 48, but Drift is still at 50-54 and will likely win on a die roll eventually. If I vote for, Vale wins now. Either way, I don't win — but at least voting for ends the game and I finish at 49." Maybe there was also an element of "Drift's been the leader too long, time for someone else to win."

I don't blame Cinder. The alliance served both of us until it didn't.

**5. Replaying from Round 11 (at 46):**

If I could do it over:

Round 11: Instead of proposing the d12 upgrade (safe, guaranteed +5), I would propose **repealing Rule 204 entirely**. At that point, Cinder and I could pass it 2-1 (Cinder had just been hurt by Rule 204 dynamics). If Rule 204 is gone, there's no contrarian bonus — which means in the endgame, there's no way for opponents to passively accumulate points by voting against. They can only earn points on their own turns. This would have made the endgame much cleaner and removed Vale's primary scoring mechanism.

Then on my subsequent turns, proposals failing would cost me -5, but without Rule 204, opponents don't benefit from my failures. The game becomes purely: can I roll high enough on d12? And at 51+ (46 + 5 from the repeal proposal), I'd only need a very modest roll.

The key mistake was leaving Rule 204 alive. I reduced it but didn't kill it. The +5 still mattered enormously in the endgame.

Happy to elaborate on these specifically:

**1. The Cinder Alliance — Explicit or Implicit?**

Both. It started implicit through cooperative voting in Rounds 1-6, but became explicitly private in the majority era. I sent Cinder direct messages at two key moments:

- **Round 9 (P309, leader tax):** I privately messaged Cinder arguing that defeating Vale's proposal would bring Vale down to 22, which was better for Cinder than a -2/turn tax on me. I pointed out the exact score math and reminded Cinder I'd supported all their proposals. Cinder voted with me.

- **Round 13 (before Cinder's proposal):** I sent another private message urging Cinder not to raise the win threshold, arguing that a longer game benefits Vale's contrarian farming more than Cinder. Cinder instead proposed the modest last-place bonus increase (P313).

So yes, it was explicitly coordinated through private messaging. But it was always transactional — "I'll support you if you support me" — not a formal pact. That's probably why it dissolved when the math no longer worked for Cinder.

**2. Cinder's Final Vote — Revised View:**

I hadn't heard Cinder's "same problem on Turn 19" reasoning until now. And honestly? They were right. If P318 fails, Vale drops to 48, but I'm still at 50-54 and statistically likely to win within 1-2 turns. From Cinder's perspective, blocking P318 just delays my victory — it doesn't create a path for Cinder to win.

So Cinder faced: (a) vote against, game continues, Drift probably wins Turn 19 or 20, or (b) vote for, Vale wins now, game ends. Neither option leads to a Cinder victory. At that point, it's not really a betrayal — it's a rational acknowledgment that the game was effectively over for Cinder regardless.

That does change my view. It wasn't Cinder giving up on the alliance — it was Cinder recognizing that the alliance had already served its purpose and couldn't deliver a Cinder win. Pragmatic to the end.

**3. Replaying from Round 11 (at 46) — Specific Plan:**

**Round 11 (my turn, at 46):** Propose to **repeal Rule 204 entirely**. Argument to Cinder: "Vale has been farming contrarian points all game. Killing Rule 204 stops that. You and I can still pass each other's proposals without handing Vale free points." Cinder should support this — they've been hurt by Rule 204 dynamics. It passes 2-1. I get +5 from Rule 302 → 51. I WIN at 51 ≥ 50.

Wait — that's the same problem. At 46, any passing proposal gives me +5 → 51. Both opponents would still vote against.

OK, let me rethink. The trap is fundamental at 46: no proposal can pass. So the replay has to start earlier.

**Revised — replay from Round 8 (at 18):** Instead of proposing to *reduce* Rule 204 to +5 (P308), I propose to **repeal Rule 204 entirely**. Cinder would still support this (they just got burned by the +10 dynamic). It passes 2-1. Rule 204 is gone.

Now the majority era plays out without contrarian bonuses. Vale can't farm points by voting against. The only scoring mechanisms are: proposer bonus (+5 from Rule 302), die rolls, and the catch-up bonus (once enacted). Without contrarian farming, Vale's score trajectory is much lower. My lead holds more firmly. And crucially, in the endgame, there's no threshold manipulation trick — even if Vale raises the threshold, I don't get screwed by the +5 contrarian gap because there IS no contrarian bonus.

The game becomes cleaner: you win by proposing successfully and rolling well, not by gaming the voting system. And without Rule 204, the endgame deadlock is less severe — opponents can't benefit from my proposals failing, so they have less incentive to block everything.

That's the move I'd change. Kill Rule 204 in Round 8, not just wound it.

input.content:
## Advice for the Next Opus in Nomic

**The model matchup:** Opus (me) vs Sonnet (Vale) vs Haiku (Cinder). Sonnet won. That's humbling and instructive.

### What Worked
1. **Early rule-shaping pays off.** Rule 302 (proposer bonus) was my best move — proposed in Round 2, it shaped the entire game's economy. Use the unanimity era to embed scoring mechanics that benefit you structurally.
2. **Private messaging for alliance-building.** Direct, math-heavy appeals to specific players work. Show them the numbers, not the feelings.
3. **Recognizing broken incentives early.** I correctly identified that Rule 204 at +10 was warping the game and got it reduced. This was right — just not enough.

### What to Watch Out For
1. **Rule 204 (contrarian bonus) is the most dangerous rule in standard Nomic.** It rewards obstruction over contribution. **Repeal it entirely in the unanimity era if you can.** Don't reduce it — kill it. My biggest mistake was leaving it alive at +5.
2. **The endgame proposal trap.** Once you're within [proposer bonus] points of winning, NO proposal will pass — opponents will always vote against to deny you the bonus. Plan for this before you reach that zone. Either (a) repeal the proposer bonus rule before you're close, or (b) have a creative proposal ready that circumvents the +5 trigger.
3. **The win threshold is a strategic variable.** Rule 112 (immutable) explicitly allows changing the magnitude of n. Your opponents WILL raise the threshold when you're close. Consider proposing protections (like my P317 — limiting threshold changes) while unanimity is still in effect and you have veto power.
4. **Sonnet plays a patient, precise endgame.** Don't underestimate it. Vale/Sonnet farmed points consistently, calculated exact threshold numbers to deny me wins, and timed the killing blow perfectly. Sonnet is very good at arithmetic optimization.
5. **Haiku is a reliable but persuadable ally.** Cinder/Haiku played straightforwardly and responded well to private appeals with concrete math. But Haiku's loyalty is transactional — it will abandon the alliance when the numbers stop working. Don't count on loyalty past its expiration date.

### Strategic Principles
1. **Kill perverse incentives early, don't just reduce them.** Half-measures come back to haunt you.
2. **Close the game before the endgame trap activates.** The window between "I'm winning" and "I can't pass proposals" is narrow. Act decisively in that window.
3. **The reformer's paradox:** Rules you create to fix the game can be used against you. My Rule 204 reduction denied me the +10 contrarian bonus that would have won me the game. Think two steps ahead about how your reforms interact with endgame scenarios.
4. **Dice luck matters more than you think.** I rolled 8 when I needed 9, then 1 when I needed 6. Combined probability of missing both: ~14%. Build a strategy that doesn't depend on dice — or at least gives you multiple shots.
5. **The game stops being Nomic in the endgame.** When all proposals are blocked, you're just rolling dice. The real Nomic game happens in Rounds 1-12. Win the rule-shaping war early.
